title: Multiplayer Basics, Profiling, and Exporting Everywhere
html:
From single-player to shipped

You have built a dozen genres. This lesson closes the loop on the two things that separate a hobby project from a real product: letting other people play with you over a network, and getting your game off your machine and onto desktops, browsers, and phones. We will keep the multiplayer scope honest — Godot's high-level multiplayer API does an enormous amount for you, and you can have two players moving in the same world in well under a hundred lines.

How Godot's high-level multiplayer works

Godot uses an authority model. Every node has a multiplayer authority (a peer ID). One machine acts as the server (peer ID 1); everyone else is a client with a unique positive ID. You set up the connection with an ENetMultiplayerPeer, then call functions across the wire with RPCs (remote procedure calls) and let MultiplayerSynchronizer nodes replicate state automatically.

net.gd (autoload)
# Autoload this as Net. Hosts or joins a game on a fixed port. extends Node const PORT := 9080 const MAX_PLAYERS := 4 var peer: ENetMultiplayerPeer func host() -> void: peer = ENetMultiplayerPeer.new() peer.create_server(PORT, MAX_PLAYERS) multiplayer.multiplayer_peer = peer print("Hosting on port " + str(PORT)) func join(ip: String) -> void: peer = ENetMultiplayerPeer.new() peer.create_client(ip, PORT) multiplayer.multiplayer_peer = peer

That is the entire networking transport. Click Host on one window and Join with 127.0.0.1 on a second, and you have a connected session.

Spawning players with MultiplayerSpawner

When a peer connects you need to give them a body. Drop a MultiplayerSpawner node in your level, set its Spawn Path to the container where players live, and register the player scene in its Auto Spawn List. On the server, instance one player per peer and the spawner replicates it to every client automatically.

level.gd
extends Node3D @onready var players := $Players func _ready() -> void: # Only the server decides who exists. if multiplayer.is_server(): multiplayer.peer_connected.connect(_add_player) multiplayer.peer_disconnected.connect(_del_player) _add_player(1) # the host plays too func _add_player(id: int) -> void: var p := preload("res://player.tscn").instantiate() p.name = str(id) # spawner matches nodes by name p.set_multiplayer_authority(id) players.add_child(p, true) func _del_player(id: int) -> void: if players.has_node(str(id)): players.get_node(str(id)).queue_free()

Authority, input, and synchronizers

The golden rule: a peer only reads input for the body it owns. Everyone else's body is a puppet whose transform arrives over the wire. Add a MultiplayerSynchronizer child to the player and add its position (and rotation) to the replication config. Then guard input with is_multiplayer_authority().

player.gd
extends CharacterBody3D const SPEED := 6.0 func _physics_process(delta: float) -> void: if not is_multiplayer_authority(): return # puppets are driven by the synchronizer var dir := Input.get_vector("left", "right", "up", "down") velocity.x = dir.x * SPEED velocity.z = dir.y * SPEED move_and_slide() # An RPC any client can call; runs on the server only. @rpc("any_peer", "call_local", "reliable") func take_hit(amount: int) -> void: print(name, " took ", amount)

The @rpc annotation is the heart of remote logic. The arguments mean: who may call it (any_peer vs authority), whether it also runs on the caller (call_local), and the channel (reliable for things that must arrive, unreliable for spammy per-frame data). To trigger it on a specific peer, use take_hit.rpc_id(target_id, 10); to broadcast, take_hit.rpc(10).

Authority law.
Never trust a client. Input and intent flow from clients to the server; the server validates, mutates the real game state, and the result replicates back. Putting damage math on the server is what stops cheaters.

Profiling and optimization

Before exporting, make it fast. Open Debugger > Profiler, press Start, and play. It shows per-frame cost of script functions and physics. The Monitors tab graphs FPS, draw calls, and memory live. Common wins:

  • Object pooling instead of instantiate() / queue_free() every frame (you did this for bullets).
  • Move heavy per-frame work out of _process — use timers or signals.
  • In 3D, reduce draw calls with fewer materials and use MultiMeshInstance3D for repeated meshes.
  • Cap framerate with Engine.max_fps and profile on the weakest target you intend to ship to.

Exporting everywhere

Open Project > Export. The first time, Godot prompts you to download the matching export templates — do it. Then Add a preset per platform:

TargetOutputNote
Windows / macOS / Linuxnative executablecodesign on macOS to avoid Gatekeeper warnings
Web (HTML5)folder of .html / .wasmneeds the Compatibility renderer; upload the folder to itch.io
Android.apk / .aabinstall the Android build template and a debug keystore

Use Export Project for a full build. For web, set the main scene in Project Settings, export to an empty folder, then zip that folder, drag it into itch.io as an HTML5 game, and tick "This file will be played in the browser."

Tip.
Web builds cannot use ENet sockets the same way native ones do. For browser multiplayer use WebSocketMultiplayerPeer or WebRTCMultiplayerPeer — the high-level API and RPCs above stay identical; only the transport class changes.
🎮 Build This: Multiplayer / Networked Co-op — Arena Together

This is a networking concept build that extends a prior game: take your top-down twin-stick arena from p1-12 and make it 2-player co-op over LAN, then export it to the web.

  1. Autoload net.gd as Net and add a tiny title menu with Host and Join buttons that call Net.host() / Net.join("127.0.0.1").
  2. Add a Players node and a MultiplayerSpawner to the arena; register the player scene and spawn one body per connected peer using the level.gd pattern.
  3. Give the player a MultiplayerSynchronizer replicating position and rotation; guard movement and aiming with is_multiplayer_authority().
  4. Convert enemy damage to a server-authoritative @rpc("authority", "call_local", "reliable") so only the host resolves hits, then broadcasts results.
  5. Run two windows locally to test, profile with the Monitors tab until you hold 60 FPS, then add a Web export preset (swap ENet for WebSocketMultiplayerPeer) and upload the HTML5 build to itch.io.

title: Capstone — Ship a Polished, Multi-Genre Portfolio Game
html:
The final build

This is it. Every node, signal, Resource, state machine, shader, and export setting you have learned now converges into one game that you will finish, polish, and publish. The goal is not the biggest game — it is a complete, shippable one. A small game that loads, plays, has audio, saves, and lives at a public URL beats a sprawling unfinished prototype every single time. Recruiters and players click links; they do not clone repos.

Choosing a capstone that shows range

Pick a scope you can finish in one focused build. A strong portfolio piece combines systems from several earlier lessons so it demonstrates breadth without being scattered. Three proven shapes:

CapstoneReuses from
Roguelike dungeon crawlertop-down combat (p1-11), AI/pathfinding (p1-19), procedural rooms (p1-22), RPG stats/inventory (p1-18)
Action platformer with bossesplatformer + AnimationTree (p1-8/9), metroidvania rooms (p1-10), shaders/VFX (p1-23)
3D arena survivalFPS/TPS controllers (p1-13/14), waves (p1-12), AI (p1-19), multiplayer (p1-25)

This Build extends your whole track. We will frame the steps around the roguelike, but the architecture applies to any choice.

Architecture: lean on what you built

Do not reinvent. Stand up a clean autoload layer first — a GameState singleton for run-wide data, an Events bus for decoupled signals, and an Audio helper. This is the same decoupling discipline from the Breakout and Snake lessons, now applied at game scale.

game_state.gd (autoload)
extends Node signal run_ended(victory: bool) var floor_index := 1 var gold := 0 var player_stats := PlayerStats.new() # a Resource from p1-18 func next_floor() -> void: floor_index += 1 get_tree().change_scene_to_file("res://floor.tscn") func end_run(victory: bool) -> void: save_high_score() run_ended.emit(victory) func save_high_score() -> void: var f := FileAccess.open("user://save.dat", FileAccess.WRITE) f.store_var({"best_floor": floor_index, "gold": gold}) f.close()

Everything else — player controller, enemies, room generator, UI — is a packed scene you have already built a version of. Capstone work is integration, not invention.

The polish that makes it feel finished

Eighty percent of "this feels like a real game" comes from a short checklist of game-feel. Pull these straight from p1-23 (VFX) and earlier juice lessons:

  • Screen shake and hit-stop on impactful hits.
  • A main menu, pause menu, and a game-over / victory screen — never dump the player straight into gameplay or a crash.
  • Music plus distinct SFX, mixed through audio buses with a settings slider.
  • Tween-based UI transitions and damage numbers; particles on death and pickups.
  • A title, an icon, and a one-line "how to play."
juice.gd (reusable helper)
extends Node # Call Juice.shake(cam, 6.0) on any impact. func shake(cam: Camera2D, strength: float) -> void: var t := create_tween() for i in 6: var off := Vector2(randf_range(-strength, strength), randf_range(-strength, strength)) t.tween_property(cam, "offset", off, 0.03) t.tween_property(cam, "offset", Vector2.ZERO, 0.05) func hit_stop(seconds: float) -> void: Engine.time_scale = 0.05 await get_tree().create_timer(seconds * 0.05, true, false, true).timeout Engine.time_scale = 1.0
Scope law.
When in doubt, cut. A finished 3-floor roguelike with menus and audio is a portfolio piece. A 30-floor one with placeholder UI and no game-over screen is not. Ship the small thing completely.

The pre-ship checklist

  1. Game starts from a menu and returns there cleanly after a run.
  2. No console errors on a fresh launch — read the Output panel line by line.
  3. Save/load survives a full quit-and-relaunch (test it for real).
  4. Profiler shows a stable 60 FPS on your weakest target.
  5. Audio has a mute/volume option; nothing clips or blasts.
  6. Exports build for Web at minimum (plus a desktop binary).

Assembling your itch.io portfolio

Create an itch.io account and a new project per game. For your capstone: upload the zipped HTML5 export, mark it browser-playable, set the embed viewport to your game's resolution, write a short description with controls, and add 3–4 screenshots and a 15-second GIF. Then make a collection or a single "portfolio" page that links every game you built across this track — Pong through the capstone. That single URL is what you put on your résumé and applications. You did not just learn Godot; you have a shelf of shipped games to prove it.

Tip.
Record a short GIF with the editor's built-in Movie Maker mode (set a target path on the main scene and run with --write-movie) or any screen recorder. Motion on a store page converts browsers into players far better than static shots.
🎮 Build This: Capstone — Your Shipped Portfolio Game

This is the capstone. It extends prior systems from the entire track rather than starting a fresh genre — you are combining combat, AI, procedural generation, stats, UI, VFX, save/load, and export into one polished, published game.

  1. Choose a capstone from the table (the roguelike is the recommended default) and create autoloads: GameState, Events, Audio, and the Juice helper above.
  2. Assemble core gameplay by reusing your strongest prior scenes — player controller, enemies with FSM/pathfinding (p1-19), and a procedural room generator (p1-22).
  3. Wire run flow: GameState.next_floor() between levels, end_run() on death/victory, and persist a high score with the save code shown.
  4. Add the full polish layer: main menu, pause, game-over/victory screens, music + SFX on buses, screen shake, hit-stop, and particles.
  5. Run the pre-ship checklist, fix every console error, then export to Web and one desktop platform.
  6. Publish the capstone on itch.io and build a single portfolio page linking every game from this track — then put that URL on your résumé.
